explanation: The statement says that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is captured by G(prop_0 -> prop_1), which states that always, if prop_0 is true at some point, then prop_1 must be true at that same point.
This is a standard implication between two atomic propositions, interpreted as: globally, whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time.
This expresses that globally, whenever 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' must also be true at that moment.
The statement means that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must be true as well. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally (always), if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds at that time.
'If prop_0, then prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is typically expressed as globally: if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is true.

This states: if prop_0 holds at any point, then prop_1 must also hold at that point. In LTL, we interpret conditional statements as implications: G(prop_0 -> prop_1), meaning 'at all times, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.'
The formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. This is the LTL translation of the implication conditional given in the natural language input.
This means that globally, whenever 'prop_0' is true at any point in time, 'prop_1' must also be true at that same point. This is expressed as: globally, 'prop_0' implies 'prop_1'.
The property states that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at that point. In LTL, this is globally (G) whenever 'prop_0' holds, it implies 'prop_1'.
This says that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In temporal logic, this is expressed globally: always, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds.
